text: The video shows a case of RTAP bilateral inguinal hernia surgery. The left inguinal hernia appears indirect, while the right side shows an irreducible fluid-containing structure in the groin. The surgeon begins by creating one large flap from right to left, without leaving a bridge in the center.

The surgeon begins the procedure on the left side, addressing the indirect inguinal hernia. The medial dissection is performed first, followed by lateral dissection. The surgeon checks the cord structures, sweeping them down and off the hernia, while demonstrating the anatomy to students, including the peritoneum extending into the hernia and the vas deferens.

The surgeon approaches the end of the peritoneal sac, ensuring complete removal as the edge becomes visible. The cord is parietalized, revealing the femoral space during inferior dissection, extending below the obturator space.
The surgeon identifies a small lipoma within the surgical field and proceeds to excise it, despite its likely inconsequential nature.

The surgeon begins addressing the right side, attempting to identify and grasp a structure within the inguinal canal. Uncertainty arises about whether the structure is part of the cord, prompting a decision to approach more medially and separate the peritoneal lining from the underlying tissues.

The surgeon encounters a thick, scarred fibrotic tissue in the groin area, attempting to isolate and sweep down a thickened tubular structure. Despite difficulty in making traction due to the dense tissue, the surgeon identifies the cord and vas, ensuring no inadvertent injury before transecting. The lump previously palpable in the groin is no longer present.

The surgeon reduces the obturator fat plug and prepares the surgical site for mesh placement. A small amount of fatty tissue is visible and reduced. The surgeon places a 16 by 12 anatomic pro grip mesh over the dissected area.

The surgeon places the left-sided mesh, ensuring the previously reduced structure is positioned behind the mesh on the peritoneal side, within the abdominal cavity. The peritoneal flap closure is performed, followed by suturing.
